signing_authority: Kaled Alhanafi, Co-founder and Chief Executive Officer

system_description.overview: Basata is a healthcare SaaS company that uses AI to automate administrative workflows for medical practices, including fax/referral intake processing, AI voice agents for call center automation, and patient scheduling. The platform assists clinicians and staff with scheduling, referral management, paperwork automation, and patient communication.

products.Automate faxes and referral intake / Adm: Processes all unstructured documents including faxes, record requests, lab orders, pharmacy or referrals. Processes new patient referrals in minutes.
products.Automate practice call centers / Voice A: Answers calls and schedules patients with AI voice agents and chatbots using practice requirements and scheduling logic.
products.Professional Services: White glove onboarding and implementation with ongoing customer support.

infrastructure.physical_presence: No physical offices; no physical servers. Headquartered at 709 E. Nighthawk Way, Phoenix, AZ 85048 (administrative only).
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ — 'spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in Azure'

network_architecture.network_segmentation: System hosted in Microsoft Azure in a VPC environment. Network topology includes segmented VPCs and ACLs. Customer accounts are segmented from each other (CC6.1.3).
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via Azure to identify and protect against threats. Intrusion detection tools configured to prevent unauthorized access and detect external threats (CC6.6.1).
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN. 'Basata VPN' visible in network diagram.
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: All systems restricted to internet via content filtering system routed through proxy server
network_architecture.topology: Client requests → app.basata.ai on Azure VM (db and storage on disk encrypted at rest) → Azure OpenAI and Document Intelligence. Inbound faxes via RingCentral (TriCity Integration), outbound calls/recordings via Twilio, inbound/outbound emails via Mailgun, Voice Transcription via OpenAI. Basata VPN for remote admin access.

application_architecture.pattern: Monolith (single Azure VM hosts app + DB + storage)

databases: Database on Azure VM disk (encrypted at rest)
file_storage: Azure VM disk storage (encrypted at rest)
data_storage.backup_frequency: Formal backup policy documented; backup procedures defined — frequency not specified

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Azure Active Directory (Active Directory mentioned for server/database accounts; Azure password policy)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1). MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3). MFA required for employee email (2FA).
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Azure password policy — periodic forced changes, expiry, complexity requirements; accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts; guest/anonymous logins not allowed

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service — 'db and storage on disk encrypted at rest' (Azure KMS)
encryption.key_management: Azure cloud provider key management service (CC6.1.5)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool (name not disclosed; MFA-protected, logged, time-stamped — likely GitHub)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured so every merge request to production requires additional approval (CC8.1.2)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configuration implementation and rollback (CC7.1.1) — tool not named
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management; CTO performs monthly patch management reviews; workstations scanned daily for patch compliance (CC7.5.1)

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Unnamed monitoring tool — 'IT team continuously monitors system capacity and performance through use of monitoring tools' (CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled for administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, function changes, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts notify IT management of high and critical risk events (CC7.2.1)

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud infrastructure, data center services, IDS/IPS, key management, OpenAI and Document Intelligence services

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software on all production servers (continuous auto-scan, weekly AV definition updates) and all desktops/laptops. Configured for Mac workstations. (CC6.8.1)
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; frequency adjusted as needed (CC7.1.2)
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party (CC4.1)

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical access controls excluded; Azure is responsible for physical data center security
A1.2 — Environmental protections excluded; Basata has no office/physical servers; managed by Azure
Processing Integrity — Determined not relevant to the system
Privacy — Determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC6.6.1: Firewalls AND intrusion detection tools both configured at application gateway and production network
CC4.1: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
CC7.1.2: Monthly automated vulnerability scans with remediation plan for critical/high findings
CC8.1.2: Branch protection requiring additional approval for every merge to production
CC6.2.2: Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged users
CC6.1.4: Access revoked within one business day upon termination
CC7.4.1: BCDR plan documented, reviewed annually, tested at least annually per defined testing schedule
Network diagram shows RingCentral, Twilio, Mailgun, OpenAI as key integrations

infrastructure.rationale: Microsoft Azure with Multi-AZ failover capability is a strong foundation, but the architecture shows a single Azure VM running app + DB + storage (monolithic), no CDN, no multi-region, and no IaC tooling named. The carve-out approach means Azure's physical controls are inherited but not audited.

application_architecture.rationale: Single Azure VM hosts the entire application stack (app + DB + storage on disk) — classic monolith. Languages, frameworks, and API style are undisclosed. Separate dev/test and prod environments exist, and branch protection is in place, but the single-VM architecture is a significant concern for a healthcare SaaS.

data_layer.rationale: Database resides on the Azure VM disk alongside the application — no separate managed database service mentioned, no caching layer, no data warehouse, no backup frequency specified beyond 'formal backup policy exists'. Data is encrypted at rest on disk.

security_posture.rationale: Strong set of controls: IDS/IPS via Azure, firewalls on application gateway + production network, anti-virus on all endpoints, MFA for admin access and source code, annual external pen test, monthly vulnerability scans, quarterly access reviews, 1-business-day access revocation, content filtering proxy. Weaknesses: no WAF explicitly, no SIEM named, no cybersecurity insurance mentioned.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection and code review enforced, separate environments, emergency change process, automated patch management with monthly reviews. However, CI/CD tool is unnamed, IaC tool is unnamed, no container orchestration, deployment approach appears manual/VM-based.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented, reviewed annually, tested at least annually per a defined testing schedule with multiple loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover via Azure. However, no RTO/RPO disclosed, no multi-region failover, and daily backup confirmation is absent.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Five identified vendors: Azure (critical), OpenAI (critical), RingCentral, Twilio, Mailgun. Azure is a single critical dependency for nearly all infrastructure. Heavy reliance on OpenAI for core AI functionality creates a second critical external dependency. Vendor management policy with quarterly risk assessments for vendors without SOC reports.

overall.rationale: Basata has a solid security process framework for a Type 1 report — annual pen testing, monthly vuln scans, MFA, quarterly access reviews, BCDR testing. The main technical concern is the single-VM architecture hosting app + DB + storage together, which lacks resilience and scalability. Healthcare data sensitivity elevates the risk of this architectural choice. The Azure carve-out means core infra controls are inherited but not directly audited.

red_flags: Single Azure VM hosts app, database, and file storage together — no separation of compute and data layers
No managed database service mentioned (e.g., Azure SQL, CosmosDB) — DB on VM disk is operationally fragile
No backup frequency disclosed despite formal backup policy
No RTO or RPO disclosed for a healthcare administrative platform
No CDN or WAF mentioned despite web-based patient scheduling and call handling
yellow_flags: CI/CD tool not named — automated deployment pipeline maturity unclear
IaC tool not named — configuration management approach unclear despite baseline config being stored as code
No SIEM tool named — log aggregation and alerting tooling undisclosed
OpenAI is a critical dependency for core AI functions — no mention of fallback or alternative
Languages and frameworks entirely undisclosed in the report
Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria excluded as 'not relevant' despite handling patient healthcare administrative data
green_flags: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation plans for critical/high findings
MFA enforced for admin access, source code access, and employee email
Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged users
Access revocation within one business day upon termination
BCDR plan documented, reviewed annually, and tested at least annually per defined testing schedule
Formal change management process with branch protection requiring additional approval for production merges
Vendor management with quarterly risk assessment for vendors lacking SOC reports
Network diagram provided showing all key integrations (RingCentral, Twilio, Mailgun, OpenAI)
key_observations: The network diagram (p.17) is unusually detailed for a SOC 2 Type 1 — it reveals the entire production topology: a single 'Basata Box' Azure VM is the core of the system, connected to all AI and communication services via VPN
The healthcare context (PHI/administrative data) combined with the single-VM architecture creates meaningful concentration risk — a single VM failure or compromise affects all services simultaneously

Azure OpenAI and Azure Document Intelligence are used within the Azure boundary (shown on same network diagram), suggesting these may be Azure-native services rather than external OpenAI API calls — this is a subtle but important architectural detail
The report explicitly excludes Processing Integrity and Privacy trust criteria as 'not relevant' — noteworthy for a healthcare AI platform processing patient referrals, prescriptions, and lab orders
Basata routes all internet-bound traffic through a content filtering proxy — unusual and noteworthy for a small startup; suggests mature network security thinking
The Accorp Partners auditor (PAC-FIRM-LIC-47383) is a common SOC 2 prep firm for early-stage startups — this is a first-time Type 1 report
